Action to perform: Describe a trait as a quality (Q) of an entity (E), a formalism widely used to describe the traits of organisms.
Why performing this action is important: The E_Q formalism allows one to identify unequivocally the trait measured (semantic disambiguation).
Examples from FAIRTraits: Each trait was modeled as a unique quality x entity combination. Qualities (i.e. area, mass, nitrogen content per dry mass) were measured on 17 entities. For example, nitrogen content per dry mass (a quality) has been measured on three root entities: absorptive roots (abr), transport roots (trr) and the whole root system (wrs), resulting in three quality x entity combinations for this specific quality: RNC_abr, RNC_trr and RNC_wrs.

Action to perform: Describe the sampling protocols (of the entity)
Why performing this action is important: Improper sampling protocol can produce erroneous results and limit the value of data. Different sampling protocols for the same trait are likely to yield different values. Follow recommendations in handbooks when available.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Full metadata on sampling protocols are provided for traits and environmental variables, at the level of each individual value. E.g. for RNC: "Whole root system collected from one or several representative individuals of a population found in full light conditions".

Action to perform: Record a unique identifier for the sample (if tracked across multiple measurements).
Why performing this action is important: Allows multiple trait measurements to be correctly linked to the same sample.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The replicate on which a specific data record was taken was identified.

Action to perform: Describe the measurement methods (of the quality)
Why performing this action is important: Improper measurement protocol can produce erroneous results and limit the value of data. Different measurement protocols for the same trait are likely to yield different values. When available, standardized protocols should be used (e.g. reference handbooks).
Examples from FAIRTraits: Full metadata on measurement protocols are provided for traits and environmental variables, at the level of each individual value. E.g. for RNC: "Elemental analyser (CHN model EA 1108 Carlo Erba Instruments [Milan - Italy]) after ginding".

Action to perform: Use acknowledged standards when available.
Why performing this action is important: Adhering to domain-relevant community standards is a key step to ensure (i) quality measurement, (ii) reproducibility, and (iii) interoperabilty and reusability. Note that some questions might require the collection of data that do not comply to existing standards; in this case, detailed information should be given so that trait data can be properly interpreted.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Standard protocols for trait measurements were generally followed (Cornelissen et al., 2003; Freschet et al., 2021); when a protocol was not available, sampling and measurement methods were detailed in specific fields of the database.

Action to perform: Identify each replicate measurement
Why performing this action is important: Needed to connect trait measurements made on the same sample (e.g., SLA and LDMC on the same leaf).
Examples from FAIRTraits: In many cases, different traits were measured on the same sample, e.g., specific leaf area and leaf dry matter content.

Action to perform: Record data at the finest resolution possible, that is, one record per trait measurement, which can even include the within-individual or within-organ level
Why performing this action is important: The decision as to which aggregation level is relevant (e.g., population or species) should be left to the end users, allowing them to assemble appropriate data for their specific research questions. This places the responsibility of recording, storing, and publishing the finest trait value resolution possible on the data creator.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The exact number of replicates differed for each of the 180 traits measured, and the finest level of replication was conserved. Consequently, raw data without any aggretation procedure can be used, while the aggregation can be conducted, for example, at the level of populations (defined as the combination of species x site x plot x treatment x “field campaign”), treatment (species x site x treatment) or species (all populations of a given species).

Action to perform: Homogenize units
Why performing this action is important: Unit is one of the minimal, essential features required to qualify continuous trait data, making data analyses possible. Units do not apply to categorical data.
Examples from FAIRTraits: For a given trait, values were homogeneized to a single unit across the whole data base, e.g. the RNC of all entities is expressed in mg.g-1.

Action to perform: Record the date and time of each samping/measurement
Why performing this action is important: Enables one to keep track of the measurements made.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The date is reported to distinguish measurements made on the same species at different times: date is one of the variables used to distinguish "populations" (i.e. a given species measured in a given site in a given treatment in spring is considered a different population than the same species in the same treatment measured in autumn).

Action to perform: Report the geolocation of each sampling/measument (if necessary)
Why performing this action is important: Needed to connect the measurements to an ecological context (if made in the field).
Examples from FAIRTraits: Latitude and longitude were reported in decimal degree, for both each site (barycenter of the site), and each measurement plot within a site.

Action to perform: Specify the full scientific name (if identified at the species level: binomial name, including genus and species, in Latin).
Why performing this action is important: Provides a standardized identifier of the organism, ensuring clarity and avoiding confusion due to vernacular names.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Binomial names were used for each species. When the identification was made at the genus level, the abbrevation sp. replaced the species name, e.g., Carex sp..

Action to perform: If taxon identified at the species level: indicate the name of the author(s) of the species name (authority)
Why performing this action is important: Ensures taxonomic precision, especially for species with homonyms or name changes
Examples from FAIRTraits: E.g., Carex humilis Leyss. 1758

Action to perform: Document the taxonomic reference used [see also B.1.2]
Why performing this action is important: Ensures consistency with current nomenclature and enables linkage to global taxonomic backbones.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The TAXREF french standard was used.

Action to perform: Provide a unique taxonomic identifier from the chosen taxonomic reference for each taxon [see also B.2.4].
Why performing this action is important: Provides stable, unique links across databases, facilitating interoperability and data reuse (B.1.2), and enables to uniquely identify the taxon within the database (B.2.4).
Examples from FAIRTraits: The TAXREF standard contains URIs (uniform resource identifiers), which were directly reused to uniquely identify the taxa.

Action to perform: Keep the original taxon names used during data collection, if they differ from the standardized names used in the final dataset.
Why performing this action is important: Helps trace historical data and avoid loss of information when names change.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Original species names (and abbreviation), used when collecting the data, were kept as verbatim species names in the final database. For instance, Bromopsis erecta (Huds.) Fourr. 1869 (name extracted from the TAXREF standard), was called Bromus erectus during fieldwork.

Action to perform: Document relevant infraspecific taxonomic rank (subspecies, variety, cultivar, genotype ID, accession number…) and provide its identifier if possible
Why performing this action is important: Captures meaningful variation below the species level
Examples from FAIRTraits: When the identification was made at the subspecies level, this finest level was identified as "subsp." (e.g. Dactylis glomerata subsp. hispanica)
Where the information is documented: [DwC] taxonRank indicates whether the scientific name given concerns a genus, a species, or a subspecies

Action to perform: Document relevant higher taxonomic information (e.g., family, order)
Why performing this action is important: Facilitates data integration and comparative analyses across broader taxonomic groups.
Examples from FAIRTraits: E.g., Carex humilis Leyss. belongs to the Plantae kingdom, and the Cyperaceae family. Note that the taxonomic information of FAIRTraits is stored in the GBIF infrasructure (see below), which gives automatically the full taxonomic hierarchy

Action to perform: Document any relevant information on the taxon that provides biological context.
Why performing this action is important: Characteristics of the taxon, often reported in floras or taxonomic databases, are crucial for interpreting trait values, understanding their variation, and enabling correct cross-species comparisons.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Each taxon is documented with its life cycle (e.g., annual, perennial), Raunkiaer life form (e.g., therophyte, phanerophyte), and other traits like photosynthetic pathway (C3, C4).
Where the information is documented: [DwC - Plinian Core Extension] LifeCycleUnstructured; LifeFormUnstructured; TrophicStrategy; DistributionUnstructured; StartTimeInterval; EndTimeInterval

Action to perform: Document any relevant information about the state of the sampled organism that could influence trait values.
Why performing this action is important: The physiological or phenological state of an individual (e.g., its developmental stage, health, or sex) can cause significant intra-specific variation in trait measurements. Documenting this context is essential for correctly interpreting the data and for reusing it in studies of phenotypic plasticity or life history.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Not done in FAIRTraits. Examples would be details on life cycle stage (e.g., 'larva', 'juvenile', 'adult'), phenological stage (e.g., 'flowering', 'fruiting'), sex, or any other pertinent individual-level characteristic.

Action to perform: Indicate the spatial organization of the observational or experimental design, including whether the data were measured in a glasshouse, a common garden, or in the field
Why performing this action is important: Trait variations can occur at small spatial scales, part of which can be understood if the spatial organisation of the studied system is described.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The spatial organisation of the sites was described, including any treatments to which plots were subjected.

Action to perform: Define the environmental variables reported
Why performing this action is important: Needed to make sense of the variables. One of the most commonly reported variable is geolocation.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Variables reported included both abiotic and biotic information. They include climate, soil, and disturbance regime descriptions.
Where the information is documented: Examples: siteMeteoStationMAT (climate: mean annual temperature); soilPlotCorg (soil: organic carbon content of soil per mass); traitPlotDisturbanceType (disturbance: qualitative type of disturbance)

Action to perform: Describe the sampling protocol for environmental variables
Why performing this action is important: Different sampling protocols for the same variable are likely to yield different values.
Examples from FAIRTraits: NA

Action to perform: Describe the measurement method for environmental variables
Why performing this action is important: Different measurement methods for the same variable are likely to yield different values.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The soilPlotCorg variable was computed as follows: "Organic content of soil = Total carbon content - mineral carbon content (Mineral carbon content = 0.12 * CaCO3 content). NF ISO 10694 Code sol-0405".

Action to perform: Specify the units of the environmental variables reported
Why performing this action is important: Unit is one of the minimal, essential features required to qualify continuous data, making data analyses possible.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The soilPlotCorg variable was expressed in g/kg.

Action to perform: Referer to a standard (if available)
Why performing this action is important: References to ontologies or thesaurus, or clear definitions (sampling protocol, measurement method, etc.) are needed to be able to use these measurements.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The soilPlotCorg variable is defined at: http://www.arras.inra.fr/pages/methode_du_las_sols.htm#Groupe4

Action to perform: Indicate the spatial scale of the measurements
Why performing this action is important: Environmental measurements can be performed at different spatial scales, from the local proximity of the plant to the site or the climatic region. The scale of the measurement must be clearly reported.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Meteorological records are given at the site level (both on a monthly and yearly basis). Soil measurements and variables describing the disturbance regimes are given at the plot level, i.e., in the direct proximity of the plants measured.

Action to perform: Specify the origin of the environmental variables (measured locally vs. extracted from databases)
Why performing this action is important: While measuring environmental variables locally is preferable, when these are not available, is it recommended to share environmental measures extracted from databases if they are relevant to understand and analyse trait data.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Environmental variables were measured either at the site or plot level (see below).

Action to perform: Identity of the origin of the measurement: experimenter(s), name of project, grants...
Why performing this action is important: Systematic error can differ among experimenters. This information can be used to control for data quality.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Each record is associated to a research project and a person responsible for the collection and cleaning of primary data

Action to perform: Provide contacts
Why performing this action is important: Necessary for any question on the data.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Available as metadata on the GBIF and InDoRES infrastructures

Action to perform: Cite publications that have used the data
Why performing this action is important: Documenting origin is necessary to avoid the same dataset, shared on different platforms, to be considered as two distinct datasets (risk of pseudo-replication in further analyses).
Examples from FAIRTraits: Available as metadata on the GBIF and InDoRES infrastructures (see section "Publishing" below). A PublishedPaper file listing the articles using subparts of the database which were published previously, together with their DOIs.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class V. F (Publications and results); Class V. G (History of data set usage)

Category2: B.1) Format and structure the data

Action to perform: Store data in a non-proprietary, machine-readable format (e.g., .txt, or .csv)
Why performing this action is important: Necessary to ensure interoperability
Examples from FAIRTraits: The .txt extension was used for the data tables.

Action to perform: Include as much information as possible within the tables
Why performing this action is important: Creates a direct connexion between trait data and their contextual data, which makes the data table more "self-sufficient", and hence more interoperable.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Trait and contextual data (sampled organism, taxa, projects) are included within the data tables.

Action to perform: Choose a structure for the data
Why performing this action is important: Depending on the heterogeneity of the data, one can decide to keep the structure used for data collection and/or data analysis, or restructure data for sharing them. Data restructuration often enables one to include more thorough data descriptions within the data table, thus connecting them directly to the data points. Direct understanding the data points contained in tables is key for data interoperability.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Original data were structured as species-trait matrices, but the final structure was a relational database, i.e. a database made of different tables which can be connected using common columns. We followed as much as possible the Ecological Trait data Standard ETS, Schneider et al., 2019), which is built as a relational database. However, as it is primarily built for taxonomic information, this standard lacks fields for environmental information. We thus extended the database with additional fields when they were missing from the standard

Action to perform: In each table, generate a unique identifier ("primary key") for each row
Why performing this action is important: Unique identifiers are necessary to uniquely identify measurements within each table
Examples from FAIRTraits: For instance, the FAIRTraits_Taxon table contains an acceptedNameUsage field which takes 240 values (the table thus has 240 rows, row titles excluded). Each value is taken once, ensuring that one, and only one, set of information is available for each taxon. This field is a primary key of the FAIRTraits_Taxon table.

Action to perform: If following a relationaldatabase structure, refer to the primary key of the extensions within the core observation table
Why performing this action is important: Referring, in the core observation table, to the primary key of each extension, is necessary to connect this core table to its extensions.
Examples from FAIRTraits: A field called acceptedNameUsage (the primary key of the taxon table, cf. recomendation S3) is also present in the FAIRTraits_TraitValues table (the core table). It contains the same values as those present in the FAIRTraits_Taxon table, but they are duplicated (the FAIRTraits_TraitValues table has 189,452 rows), because mulitple measurements were made on the each taxon. This column is called a foreign key of the FAIRTraits_TraitValues table. It is used to connect information present in the Taxon table to that present in the TraitValues table. The same logic applies to the other tables (e.g., environmental information).

Action to perform: Rely on a standard to define variable/column names (if possible).
Why performing this action is important: Following a data standard with unambiguous field (i.e., column) names makes data structuration easier to understand by data reusers and to combine (manually or automatically) to other data, which are determinant contributions to the interoperability of datasets.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The Darwin Core standard was used for taxonomic and trait data. However, this standard did not manage the associated environmental information, which was thus documented by adapting the Ecological Trait data Standard. Both standards are compatible with the structure of relational databases, i.e., tables connected together by common columns.

Action to perform: Describe precisely the variables not found in standards (when documenting the general metadata).
Why performing this action is important: Standards might not be adapted to all data, in particular when data are very original in their content.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Each variable not defined in standards was defined precisely. This was the case, for example, for soil and disturbance characteristics

Action to perform: Standardize trait names and methods by mapping them to persistent identifiers from community-accepted ontologies.
Why performing this action is important: This practice is fundamental for interoperability, allowing different datasets to be unambiguously combined and understood by automated systems. Examples of ontologies include the Trait Ontology (TO) for traits, or the Methods Ontology for procedures.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Terminological resources were used to standardize trait semantics (Garnier et al., 2017; Ely et al., 2021); original definitions were coined when necessary and detailed in a specific field of the database.

Action to perform: Standardize taxonomic names by mapping them to persistent identifiers from a community-accepted source (cf. A.2.3)
Why performing this action is important: Ensures correct species identification and links to global taxonomic resources. This is necessary for combining different data sets, which may name the same taxa differently.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The TAXREF taxonomic reference database (https://taxref.mnhn.fr/), used in FAIRTraits, is itself used in larger taxonomies, thus providing interoperability.

Action to perform: Standardise environmental measurements by referencing relevant ontologies or thesauri (cf. A.3.5)
Why performing this action is important: Allows for the integration of environmental context across different studies.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The environmental ontology (ENVO, https://sites.google.com/site/environmentontology/) was referred to whenever possible

Action to perform: Favour algorithmic over manual restructuration
Why performing this action is important: Keeps raw data intact and ensures all transformations are explicit, traceable, and reproducible.
Examples from FAIRTraits: All the steps used to restructure (and complete) raw data into the final database were performed using of scripts which took as input (1) raw data, and (2) new data tables designed to add missing information (related to data content or data structure). For instance, the FAIRTraits_TraitValues table is built from 1) the raw species-trait matrices, and 2) a table filled manually which documents the measurement methods for each trait, in each context. The restructuration and merging of the two files generates the final FAIRTraits_TraitValues table.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class V. D: Computer programs and processing algorithms

Action to perform: Use a version control system
Why performing this action is important: Versioning of the scripts makes it possible to go back to previous versions of the data restructuration process if necessary.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The git version control software was used, and the scripts were uploaded to GitHub to enable collaboration between people working on the database, and script archiving.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class V. D: Computer programs and processing algorithms

Action to perform: Share the code used to restructure data
Why performing this action is important: Sharing the code ensures the data processing is transparent, reproducible, and allows others to build upon your methodology.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The scripts and the whole versioning history was made available on GitHub. An additional Zenodo DOI was generated for permanency, versioning, and citation requirements. The scripts were written in the R language, with documentation of the packages used and their versions using the R sessionInfo() function. A README was used to document the scripts structures.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class V. D: Computer programs and processing algorithms

Action to perform: Associate a usage license to the scripts
Why performing this action is important: Needed to explicitly state the conditions under which the scripts can be reused/modified.
Examples from FAIRTraits: A MIT (open source) license was applied to the scripts.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class V. D: Computer programs and processing algorithms

Category2: C.1) Publish a metadata file in a standard format

Action to perform: Use a community-approved metadata standard
Why performing this action is important: Using a metadata standard (1) ensures that all key aspects of the metadata are effectively documented, and (2) favours the interoperability with other datasets documented using the same metadata standard. A best practice is to adopt widely recognized detailed metadata standards, such as EML or ISO 19115 for ecological data.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The Ecological Metadata Standard (EML) was used to document the FAIRTraits database, as the standard used in the GBIF infrastructure (see below)
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class IV.B (data structural descriptors - variable information)

Action to perform: Publish the metadata to a searchable repository or catalog.
Why performing this action is important: Metadata are the proxy by which data are searched.
Examples from FAIRTraits: EML (Ecological Metadata Language) metadata were associated to the data archive on GBIF, and the Dataverse metadata standard to the InDoRES archive, both of which are catalogs with search engines. Additionally, FAIRTraits has been released as a data paper, favouring its discovery by other researchers.

Category2: C.2) Publish a rich metadata file

Action to perform: Make a general description of the dataset (title, originators, abstract, key words)
Why performing this action is important: Necessary to have a general glimpse of the dataset, and for querying with search engines.
Examples from FAIRTraits: This description is required in the EML standard. Additionally, a data paper, FAIRTraits, describing the dataset, has been published in Ecology.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class I (Data set descriptors) and Class II (Research origin descriptors)

Action to perform: Describe the data content (trait and taxonomic coverage, etc.) and structure (tables, variables, relationships)
Why performing this action is important: Metadata must document the exact structuration of the data, generally into tables, and describe each field (i.e., column name) of the tables, for the users to be able navigate the data.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The database was structured into a TraitValues and a Taxon table, with extensions documenting the environment. This is fully described in the data paper. See also B.2.

Action to perform: Document the quality control procedures applied [cf. Section E]
Why performing this action is important: Quality control is necessary to confidently use data and are a criterion when searching for datasets. Details of how this control was made can further guide data analysis.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Cf. section D on quality control below.

Action to perform: Provide information on accessibility (repositories, persistent identifier, license usage) [cf. Section D]
Why performing this action is important: This accessibility information (the 'where' and 'how') must be recorded in the metadata itself to enable discovery and access.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Cf. section C.2 above

Action to perform: Disseminate the information about the data publication (and possibly write a data paper)
Why performing this action is important: Using multiple channels to inform on data publication enhances its findability.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The FAIRTraits database is fully described in a data paper, shared on social media platforms.
Where the information is documented: Data paper published in Ecology 106: e70219. doi: 10.1002/ecy.70219

Category2: D.1) Preserve and license the data package

Action to perform: Archive data in a long-term repository committed to FAIR practices, queriable by both humans and machines, and associate its metadata.
Why performing this action is important: Ensures that data will be available on the long-term.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Data were uploaded to the GBIF (https://www.gbif.org/) and the InDoRES (https://www.indores.fr/) infrastructures, both of which benefit from long-term support. Both GBIF and InDoRES include a search engine and are regularly scanned by web search engines.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class III: Dataset status and accessibility; Class V.E: Archiving

Action to perform: Upload metadata together with the data
Why performing this action is important: Metadata are necessary to make sense of the data (see above).
Examples from FAIRTraits: Metadata were associated to the data archive on GBIF, and the Dataverse metadata standard to the InDoRES archive.

Action to perform: Assign a persistent identifier (DOI) to the dataset.
Why performing this action is important: A unique and persistent identifier is necessary for the data to be findable.
Examples from FAIRTraits: DOIs have been attributed to the data archives present on GBIF and InDoRES.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class III: Dataset status and accessibility; Class V.E: Archiving

Action to perform: Specify reusability conditions with a a clear usage license
Why performing this action is important: Necessary for data reuse.
Examples from FAIRTraits: CC-BY 4.0 licenses were associated to the archives.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class III: Dataset status and accessibility

Category2: D.2) Preserve and license the processing scripts

Action to perform: Preserve the processing code/scripts to a repository (with a DOI and license).
Why performing this action is important: Srcipts inform on the procedures implemented to control data and to make it interoperable
Examples from FAIRTraits: Although GitHub repositories do not have DOIs by default, an archive can be automatically generated from the repository and uploaded to Zenodo with a DOI. This was done: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17073323
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class V.E: Computer programs and data processing algorithms

Action to perform: Check data structure
Why performing this action is important: Consistency of the data structure is necessary to reuse the data. Controlling that the row number is as expected helps identifying (partial) duplications. When data is structured in different tables, their correspondence must be verified, i.e., the common columns between the tables must contain the same information.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Unicity of the values taken by the primary keys of each table were controlled. The correspondence between variables (e.g., trait name and measurement method) was controlled manually by verifying all the combinations of these variables. The exact matching between columns present in different tables was controlled for by merging the tables and searching for incorrect merges.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class V. B: Quality assurance/quality control procedures

Action to perform: Check numeric variables for outliers and plausible ranges.
Why performing this action is important: Outlier detection allows one to detect values that are very different from the other values of the data distribution. They may reveal (1) errors easy to correct (e.g., unit error, wrongly placed decimal); (2) biological variability to be kept in the dataset, or (3) an error which requires removing the data point.
Examples from FAIRTraits: Outlier detection was automated based on a distance index from trait measurements of individuals of the same species. The final decision on whether to (1) correct, (2) keep, or (3) remove the data point was made by humans, as deciding whether an outlier most likely results from biological variability or measurement errors is difficult to do algorithmically, but can be made based on expert knowledge.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class V. B: Quality assurance/quality control procedures

Action to perform: Check units for homogeneity across the dataset.
Why performing this action is important: Divergent units for the same trait across subparts of datasets can be an important source of errors.
Examples from FAIRTraits: For a given trait, values were homogeneized to a single unit across the whole data base. When an outlier was detected, possible unit errors were first investigated.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class V. B: Quality assurance/quality control procedures

Action to perform: Check non-numeric variables (e.g., taxon names, identifiers) for misspellings and consistency.
Why performing this action is important: Non-numeric variables (e.g., species name, individual identifier, etc.) are used to connect data points together (e.g., measurements made on the same species, or trait and environmental measurements). They must be carefully checked for homogeneity across the database, as small mispelling errors can prevent connecting these data points.
Examples from FAIRTraits: All values taken by each variable were individually exported and controlled for potential duplications due to misspellings. For instance, a taxa list was exported and misspellings were controlled for.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class V. B: Quality assurance/quality control procedures

Action to perform: Ensure that URLs pointing to external references are functional
Why performing this action is important: Functional links are a prerequisite for accessing the external resources cited in the database.
Examples from FAIRTraits: All references to online resources were checked before releasing the database.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class V. B: Quality assurance/quality control procedures

Action to perform: Ensure reproducibility of the quality control and correction procedures
Why performing this action is important: Many errors can occur when correcting data values and restructuring data. It is thus absolutely necessary to keep track of which changes were made, and to be able to go back to previous versions if these corrections turn out to be irrelevant.
Examples from FAIRTraits: See above (B.3, data restructuration)
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class V. B: Quality assurance/quality control procedures; Class V. D: Computer programs and processing algorithms

Action to perform: Proofread the metadata files and control for errors.
Why performing this action is important: Metadata is the first access to a dataset, and must thus be exhaustive and correct so that potential data reusers have an accurate assessment of the dataset content, structure, and accessibility.
Examples from FAIRTraits: The EML file was completed by the lead author, and proof-read by the coauthors.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class V. B: Quality assurance/quality control procedures; Class V. D: Computer programs and processing algorithms

Action to perform: Verify the published data package matches the intended release, and pay particular attention to control the presence of DOIs and licenses.
Why performing this action is important: Ensures that the correct, final version of all files (data, metadata, scripts) has been uploaded and that no errors occurred during the publication process. Additionally ensures that two elements of paramount importance for accessibility (DOIs and license) are present.
Examples from FAIRTraits: After uploading to the repository, the published package was downloaded and verified for file integrity, completeness. The data set was reopened and read correctly.
Where the information is documented: [EML] Class V. B: Quality assurance/quality control procedures; Class V. D: Computer programs and processing algorithms
